title: SQL Lab 11: Customers with No Orders
short_description: Find customers with no orders using a NULL-safe anti-join pattern.
business_context: Marketing teams need to identify customers who have never ordered, but NULLs in order data can break NOT IN logic.
problem_statement: Return customers who never placed an order. The orders table may contain NULL customer_id values.
student_task: Write a query returning customer_id and customer_name.
learning_goals: Understand why NOT IN can fail with NULLs
Write anti-joins that survive dirty source data
production_logs: campaign-build: no customers returned even though inactive customers exist
solution_query:
SELECT c.customer_id,
       c.customer_name
FROM customers c
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM orders o
  WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id
);
explanation: NOT EXISTS checks for a matching order per customer and is not poisoned by NULL values in the orders subquery.
common_mistakes: Using NOT IN when the subquery can return NULL
Filtering on the wrong side of a left join
Returning order columns instead of customer columns
hints: The orders table has a NULL customer_id.
NOT IN behaves badly when the subquery contains NULL.
Use NOT EXISTS or a left anti-join.
